question: Which loop is guaranteed to execute at least once?
tip: Loops are the engine of your code. In CS211, you will use them for complex tasks like searching through data and building Linked Lists (Ch16). If you struggle with loops now, advanced topics like Recursion (Ch12) and Sorting (Ch13) will be very difficult to follow. Practice nesting loops and using for-each to build your confidence.

question: How are primitive types passed in Java?
tip: Methods organize your logic, and understanding how data moves between them is critical. Java's 'Pass-by-Value' system is the foundation for Recursion (Ch12) and managing Binary Trees (Ch17). If parameters and return types are confusing, your code will likely have bugs that are hard to find. Focus on how object references differ from primitive numbers when passed into a method.

question: When passing an object to a method, what is passed by value?
options: A full copy of the object
The memory reference to the object
answer: The memory reference to the object

question: Which follows Boolean Zen?
tip: Logic flow is how your program makes decisions. 'Boolean Zen' is about writing clean, simple logic without extra '== true' statements. In CS211, you'll need sharp logic to navigate complex structures like Binary Search Trees (Ch17) and Hash Tables (Ch18). Mastering clean if-else statements now will prevent massive headaches when debugging later.

question: What is the index of the first element in an array?
tip: Arrays and ArrayLists are the most common ways to store data in Java. CS211 is all about learning how these work 'under the hood' to create Stacks and Queues (Ch14). question: Which keyword is used for class inheritance?
tip: Inheritance and Interfaces allow you to share code between different parts of your project. This is a major focus in the Ch09 Review and is used to build flexible, professional software. question: What is the key characteristic of a HashSet?
tip: A HashSet is a super-fast way to store unique items without duplicates. It is much faster than a standard list for checking if an item exists (O(1) complexity). In CS211, you will learn how to build your own version of this using Hashing (Ch18). Knowing when to use a Set instead of a List is a sign of a true intermediate programmer.

question: What does a HashMap store?
tip: HashMaps are incredibly powerful tools that link 'Keys' to 'Values' for instant retrieval. They are used everywhere in professional software for things like dictionaries or databases. In CS211, you'll explore how these are built using arrays and linked lists (Ch16-18). Mastering HashMaps now gives you a massive advantage for the final projects of the quarter.
